Desperate for a companion, he carved a piece of his own heart, a sphere of cool, white marble, and cast it into the velvet darkness. This was Selene, the Moon.

Only during the eclipse do they finally meet. Long ago, before the sky had eyes, the Night was a thick, suffocating blanket that terrified the creatures of the earth. They huddled in caves, fearing the unseen beasts that prowled the darkness.

One evening, a young weaver named Elara walked to the edge of the world, holding a single, glowing ember from her hearth. She did not fear the dark; she pitied it for its loneliness. Standing upon the highest cliff, she began to spin the smoke from her ember into a fine, silver thread. With nimble fingers, she wove the thread into a great, luminous tapestry, pouring her warmth and hope into the fabric.

When the tapestry was finished, it was a perfect, glowing circle. Elara tossed it high into the velvet blackness. It stuck fast, becoming the Moon. But her work was not done. In her excitement, she had pricked her finger, and drops of her blood scattered across the sky. Where they landed, they crystallized into stars.

Now, the Night is no longer a blind terror but a canvas of silver light. The creatures no longer hide, for Elara’s lantern watches over them, reminding them that even in the deepest dark, a single spark can weave a galaxy.
